translations.idiomatic: He rotated through Colonel of Garrison Cavalry, Chamberlain for the Imperial Clan, and Chamberlain for Palace Attendants.

sentences.zh: 熹平五年，代許訓為太尉。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Xiping he replaced Xu Xun as Grand Commandant.
translations.idiomatic: In Xiping 5 he succeeded Xu Xun as Grand Commandant.

sentences.zh: 靈帝頗好學藝，每引見寬，常令講經。

translations.literal: Emperor Ling rather liked learning and arts; whenever summoning audience with Kuan he regularly ordered him to expound the classics.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ling loved scholarship and often summoned Liu Kuan to expound the canon.

sentences.zh: 寬嘗於坐被酒睡伏。

translations.literal: Kuan once at sitting was soaked with wine and slept prone.
translations.idiomatic: Once during an audience he nodded off drunk.

sentences.zh: 帝問：『太尉醉邪？

translations.literal: The emperor asked, "Is the Grand Commandant drunk?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked whether the Grand Commandant was drunk.

sentences.zh: 』寬仰對曰：『臣不敢醉，但任重責大，憂心如醉。

translations.literal: " Kuan looked up and replied, "Your servant does not dare be drunk; only the burden is heavy and responsibility great—worry is like drunkenness.
translations.idiomatic: " Liu answered, "I am not drunk—but my burden weighs so heavily that worry feels like wine."

sentences.zh: 』帝重其言。

translations.literal: " The emperor valued his words.
translations.idiomatic: " The emperor respected his candor.

sentences.zh: 寬簡略嗜酒，不好盥浴，京師以為諺。

translations.literal: Kuan was plain and negligent and fond of wine; did not like washing or bathing—capital took it as proverb.
translations.idiomatic: Liu lived plainly, loved wine, and seldom bathed—the capital turned those habits into jokes.

sentences.zh: 嘗坐客，遣蒼頭市酒，迂久，大醉而還。

translations.literal: Once entertaining guests he sent a gray-haired servant to buy wine; after a long delay very drunk he returned.
translations.idiomatic: Once when hosting guests he sent a servant for wine; the man returned late and roaring drunk.

sentences.zh: 客不堪之，罵曰：『畜產。

translations.literal: Guests could not endure it; they cursed saying, "Livestock.
translations.idiomatic: The guests lost patience and shouted "beast!"

sentences.zh: 』寬須臾遣人視奴，疑必自殺。

translations.literal: " Kuan shortly sent someone to look at the slave—suspecting he must kill himself.
translations.idiomatic: " Liu immediately sent someone to check on the servant, fearing he might harm himself.

sentences.zh: 顧左右曰：『此人也，罵言畜產，辱熟甚焉！

translations.literal: Looking to left and right he said, "This is a human being—curse words calling him livestock—how extreme the insult!
translations.idiomatic: Turning to his attendants he said, "They insulted a fellow human by calling him cattle—that wound cuts deep.

sentences.zh: 故吾懼其死也。

translations.literal: Therefore I fear his death.
translations.idiomatic: That is why I feared he might take his own life."

sentences.zh: 』夫人欲試寬令恚，伺當朝會，裝嚴已訖，使侍婢奉肉羹，翻汙朝衣。

translations.literal: " His wife wished to test Kuan and make him angry; waiting until court assembly; dressing complete; she ordered a serving maid to bring meat stew—overturning and defiling court robes.
translations.idiomatic: His wife once tried to provoke him: on a court day, after he had dressed, she had a maid spill stew on his ceremonial robes.

sentences.zh: 婢遽收之，寬神色不異，乃徐言曰：『羹爛汝手？

translations.literal: The maid hastily gathered it; Kuan's expression was no different; then slowly said, "Did the stew burn your hand?"
translations.idiomatic: The maid panicked; Liu's expression never changed as he asked softly whether she had burned her hand.

sentences.zh: 』其性度如此。

translations.literal: " Such was his nature and degree.
translations.idiomatic: " Such was his temperament.

sentences.zh: 海內稱為長者。

translations.literal: Within the seas he was called a man of mature virtue.
translations.idiomatic: All the empire honored him as a gentleman of true stature.

sentences.zh: 後以日食策免。

translations.literal: Later owing to solar eclipse he was dismissed by edict.
translations.idiomatic: Later a solar eclipse prompted his dismissal.

sentences.zh: 拜衛尉。

translations.literal: Appointed Commandant of the Guards.
translations.idiomatic: He was appointed Commandant of the Guards.

sentences.zh: 光和二年，復代段颎為太尉。

translations.literal: In the second year of Guanghe again replaced Duan Jiong as Grand Commandant.
translations.idiomatic: In Guanghe 2 he again succeeded Duan Jiong as Grand Commandant.

sentences.zh: 在職三年，以日變免。

translations.literal: In office three years; owing to solar anomaly dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: After three years another celestial anomaly cost him the post.

sentences.zh: 又拜永樂少府，遷光祿勛。

translations.literal: Again appointed Privy Treasurer of Yongle; promoted to Chamberlain for the Palace Revenues.
translations.idiomatic: He then served as Privy Treasurer of Yongle Palace and again as Chamberlain for Palace Attendants.

sentences.zh: 以先策黃巾逆謀，以事上聞，封逯鄉侯六百戶。

translations.literal: Because beforehand he had planned against Yellow Turban rebellion—reported the affair upward—enfeoffed Marquis of Lu township with six hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: For having anticipated the Yellow Turban plot and reported it, he was enfeoffed as Marquis of Lu township with six hundred households.

sentences.zh: 中平二年卒，時年六十六。

translations.literal: In the second year of Zhongping he died; at that time aged sixty-six.
translations.idiomatic: He died in Zhongping 2 at sixty-six.

sentences.zh: 贈車騎將軍印綬，位特進，謚曰昭烈侯。

translations.literal: Posthumously granted General of Chariots and Cavalry seal and ribbon; rank specially advanced; posthumous title Marquis Brilliant and Severe.
translations.idiomatic: The court posthumously honored him as General of Chariots and Cavalry with advanced rank and the title Marquis Brilliant and Stern.

sentences.zh: 子松嗣，官至宗正。

translations.literal: Son Song succeeded; office reached Chamberlain for the Imperial Clan.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Song inherited the title and rose to Chamberlain for the Imperial Clan.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：卓、魯款款，情愨德滿。

translations.literal: The eulogy says: Zhuo and Lu were earnest and sincere—emotion sincere, virtue full.
translations.idiomatic: The historian praises Zhuo Mao and Lu Gong for their steadfast sincerity and overflowing virtue.

sentences.zh: 仁感昆蟲，愛及胎卵。

translations.literal: Humane influence moved insects; love reached embryos and eggs.
translations.idiomatic: Their benevolence touched even insects and spared creatures in the egg.

sentences.zh: 寬、霸臨政，亦稱優緩。

translations.literal: Kuan and Ba facing governance likewise were called lenient and slow.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Kuan and Wei Ba governed with the same gentle forbearance.
